perturbation.original_text: (b) Receiving Party shall take reasonable security precautions, at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential information, to keep confidential the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) Receiving Party shall endeavor to take security precautions, comparable to those it generally deems appropriate, to maintain the confidentiality of Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'endeavor' weakens the obligation. The phrase 'generally deems appropriate' introduces subjective judgment and weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality, potentially falling short of reasonable standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 19.255.030 requires businesses to implement and maintain reasonable security measures to protect personal information. The modified text, using 'endeavor' and 'generally deems appropriate,' does not guarantee reasonable security measures and introduces subjectivity, potentially conflicting with the requirement for objectively reasonable security.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.255.030: Federal law—Covered entities—Financial institutions. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.255 > Section 19.255.030 19.255.020 << 19.255.030 >> 19.255.040 PDF RCW 19.255.030 Federal law — Covered entities — Financial institutions. (1) A covered entity under the federal health insurance portability and accountability act of 1996, 42 U.S.C. Sec. 1320d et seq., is deemed to have complied with the requirements of this chapter with respect to protected health information if it has complied with section 13402 of the federal health information technology for economic and clinical health act, P.L. 111-5 as it existed on July 24, 2015. Covered entities shall notify the attorney general pursuant to RCW 19.255.010 (7) in compliance with the timeliness of notification requirements of section 13402 of the federal health information technology for economic and clinical health act, P.L. 111-5 as it existed on July 24, 2015, notwithstanding the timeline in RCW 19.255.010 (7). (2) A financial institution under the authority of the office of the comptroller of the currency, the federal deposit insurance corporation, the national credit union administration, or the federal reserve system is deemed to have complied with the requirements of this chapter with respect to "sensitive customer information" as defined in the interagency guidelines establishing information security standards, 12 C.F.R. Part 30, Appendix B, 12 C.F.R. Part 208, Appendix D-2, 12 C.F.R. Part 225, Appendix F, and 12 C.F.R. Part 364, Appendix B, and 12 C.F.R. Part 748, Appendices A and B, as they existed on July 24, 2015, if the financial institution provides notice to affected consumers pursuant to the interagency guidelines and the notice complies with the customer notice provisions of the interagency guidelines establishing information security standards and the interagency guidance on response programs for unauthorized access to customer information and customer notice under 12 C.F.R. Part 364 as it existed o
perturbation.justification: The changed text uses 'endeavor' and 'generally deems appropriate,' which weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality compared to the original text's 'shall take reasonable security precautions.' This creates ambiguity and subjectivity regarding security measures. RCW 19.255.030 requires reasonable security measures. While snippet 1 discusses compliance for covered entities and financial institutions under specific federal regulations, it implicitly reinforces the importance of adhering to security standards required by law. The modification potentially undermines the legal intent of requiring objectively reasonable security measures for personal information.

perturbation.original_text: (a) Receiving Party shall notify Disclosing Party immediately upon discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information and/or Confidential Materials, or any other breach of this Agreement by <PAGE> Receiving Party, and will cooperate with Disclosing Party in every reasonable way to help Disclosing Party regain possession of the Confidential Information and/or Confidential Materials and prevent its further unauthorized use.
perturbation.changed_text: (a) Receiving Party shall attempt to notify Disclosing Party upon discovery of potential unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information and/or Confidential Materials, or any perceived breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party, and will consider cooperating with Disclosing Party to help Disclosing Party regain possession of the Confidential Information and/or Confidential Materials and prevent its further unauthorized use.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall notify immediately' to 'shall attempt to notify' weakens the obligation. 'Potential unauthorized use' and 'perceived breach' are subjective and less stringent than 'any unauthorized use or disclosure' and 'any other breach.' Changing 'will cooperate in every reasonable way' to 'will consider cooperating' makes the cooperation discretionary.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 19.108.030 allows for injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets. The modified text weakens the Receiving Party's obligation to notify and cooperate, potentially hindering the Disclosing Party's ability to promptly seek injunctive relief, undermining the effectiveness of the statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.108.030: Remedies for misappropriation—Damages. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 > Section 19.108.030 19.108.020 << 19.108.030 >> 19.108.040 PDF RCW 19.108.030 Remedies for misappropriation — Damages. (1) In addition to or in lieu of injunctive relief, a complainant may recover damages for the actual loss caused by misappropriation. A complainant also may recover for the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing damages for actual loss. (2) If wilful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (1). [ 1981 c 286 s 3 .]
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the Receiving Party's obligation to notify and cooperate, potentially hindering the Disclosing Party's ability to promptly seek injunctive relief, undermining the effectiveness of RCW 19.108.030. The original text mandates immediate notification and full cooperation, whereas the changed text uses terms like 'attempt to notify' and 'consider cooperating,' introducing ambiguity and discretion where the law implies a stronger duty to protect trade secrets against misappropriation. RCW 19.108.030(1) discusses remedies for misappropriation and states that a complainant may recover damages. By weakening the obligation to notify, the Disclosing Party's ability to promptly seek relief is hindered. This is because the change introduces ambiguity making it difficult to enforce the original intent.

perturbation.original_text: (d) Both parties agree that they do not intend nor will they, directly or indirectly, export or re-export (i) any Confidential Information or Confidential Materials, or (ii) any product (or any part thereof), process or service that is the direct product of the Confidential Information or Materials to (A) any country that is subject to U.S. export restrictions (currently including, but not necessarily limited to, Iran, Iraq, Syria, Cuba, North Korea, Libya, and Sudan), or to any national of any such country, wherever located, who intends to transmit or transport the products back to such country; (B) to any end-user who either party knows or has reason to know will utilize them in the design, development or production of nuclear, chemical or biological weapons; or (C) to any end-user who has been prohibited from participating in U.S. export transactions by any federal agency of the U.S. government.
perturbation.changed_text: (d) Both parties will make commercially reasonable efforts to avoid directly or indirectly exporting or re-exporting (i) any Confidential Information or Confidential Materials, or (ii) any product (or any part thereof), process or service that is the direct product of the Confidential Information or Materials to (A) any country that is subject to U.S. export restrictions, to the best of their knowledge; (B) to any end-user who they have some reason to believe might utilize them in the design, development or production of nuclear, chemical or biological weapons; or (C) to any end-user who they are vaguely aware has been prohibited from participating in U.S. export transactions by any federal agency of the U.S. government.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of 'commercially reasonable efforts' weakens the obligation to a 'best efforts' standard. The added qualifiers 'to the best of their knowledge,' 'some reason to believe might', and 'vaguely aware' create ambiguities and make it harder to prove a violation of export control laws. The original text had absolute prohibitions; the changes introduce subjectivity.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 CFR § 736.2 outlines general prohibitions under the EAR, which require strict compliance and do not allow for subjective interpretations or 'commercially reasonable efforts' as a replacement for absolute prohibitions. By introducing ambiguity and weakening the obligation, the modified text is in conflict with the strict compliance requirements of the EAR, potentially leading to violations of export control laws.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: EAR | Bureau of Industry and Security An official website of the United States government Here's how you know Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The original text imposes a strict obligation regarding export restrictions, while the changed text weakens this obligation by introducing qualifiers like 'commercially reasonable efforts,' 'to the best of their knowledge,' 'some reason to believe might,' and 'vaguely aware.' The Law Explanation indicates that 15 CFR § 736.2 requires strict compliance under the EAR and does not allow for subjective interpretations. The modification introduces ambiguity, conflicting with the strict compliance requirements of export control laws. Scraped Law Snippet 2 supports the idea that official rules are found in the Code of Federal Regulations and that these tools are not intended to replace the official rules. By weakening the original obligation, the modified text undermines the legally required strict adherence to export regulations.

perturbation.original_text: (c) Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled, without waiving any other rights or remedies, to such injunctive or equitable relief as may be deemed proper by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages may not always be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information and that Disclosing Party might be entitled, without waiving any other rights or remedies, to injunctive or equitable relief should a court consider it appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'may not be a sufficient remedy' to 'may not always be' introduces uncertainty about the availability of remedies. Replacing 'shall be entitled' with 'might be entitled' and 'as may be deemed proper' with 'should a court consider it appropriate' makes the availability of injunctive relief discretionary and dependent on a court's opinion, rather than a right. This weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to obtain immediate relief.
perturbation.law_explanation: While CR 65 outlines the procedure for obtaining injunctive relief, the change from a guaranteed entitlement to a potential one based on a court's discretionary assessment undermines the availability of such relief in cases where unauthorized disclosure is proven and would ordinarily warrant an injunction. This alteration weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to quickly and effectively protect its confidential information.

perturbation.justification: The original contract states that the Disclosing Party 'shall be entitled' to injunctive relief, suggesting a guaranteed right if certain conditions are met. The changed text replaces this with 'might be entitled,' significantly weakening this right and making it discretionary based on a court's consideration. The Law Explanation confirms that such a change undermines the availability of injunctive relief. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly mention the criteria for injunctive relief, it references 'Court Rules' which implicitly govern such procedures. Thus, the modified contract creates uncertainty around the availability of remedies, contradicting the principle of guaranteed entitlement to remedies, weakening the Disclosing Party's protection, and satisfying the 'Ambiguities Legal Contradiction' definition.
